skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate, animate, upscale, merge, and add sound to AI videos through the inference.sh CLI and supported video models.
skill.summary: Run AI video generation and editing workflows through inference.sh CLI commands.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: The static external command findings are mostly Markdown backtick false positives, but the skill intentionally permits the inference.sh CLI through Bash(infsh *). Network URLs are expected for this video-generation workflow, and no prompt injection, weak cryptography, or malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Execution Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash(infsh *) and documents infsh app run workflows. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but prompts and referenced media can be sent to external model services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool and examples clearly show intended CLI execution. The behavior is expected, but it creates a privacy and supply-chain review requirement for a community skill.

medium_findings.title: Remote Media URL Handling
medium_findings.description: Several examples pass image_url, audio_url, or video_url values to remote video applications. Users should avoid sending private media URLs unless they trust the inference.sh service and selected providers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include remote media URL inputs for external processing. No hidden exfiltration was found, but the data-sharing behavior is central to the workflow.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography at lines that contain metadata prose and a table heading. No hash, cipher, signature, or cryptographic operation is present in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows descriptive text and Markdown table structure only. There is no executable code or cryptographic API usage at the flagged lines.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are primarily inline Markdown code spans and fenced command examples. They document model IDs and CLI usage rather than executing shell code inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around examples and identifiers. The only meaningful execution risk is the intentional infsh CLI permission handled in the medium findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Scope for infsh
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access scoped to infsh commands. This should remain restricted to infsh and should not be broadened without another security review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and directly controls command access. The scope is narrow, but still permits external CLI actions.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Documentation and Media Links
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes links to inference.sh documentation and sample remote media URLs. These are expected, but they confirm a network-dependent workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible and related to the service described by the skill. No deceptive endpoint or credential exfiltration pattern was found.

content.user_title: Generate AI Videos with CLI Workflows
content.value_statement: AI video work often requires switching between many model tools and formats. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused inference.sh workflow for generation, animation, upscaling, lipsync, and media merging.
actual_capabilities: Runs text-to-video jobs with listed models such as Veo, Grok Video, Seedance, WAN-T2V, and P-Video.
Animates images through image-to-video models such as Wan 2.5, WAN-I2V, P-Video, and Seedance Lite.
Creates avatar and lipsync workflows with OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse examples.
Documents utility workflows for video upscaling, foley sound generation, and video merging.
Shows how to browse available video applications through the inference.sh CLI.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends prompts, media URLs, and job inputs to external services selected by inference.sh.
Does not include local video rendering, editing, or offline model execution.
Output quality, cost, speed, and availability depend on each remote model provider.

use_cases.title: Create Short Marketing Clips
use_cases.description: Generate product or campaign videos from written prompts, then refine model choice for speed or quality.

use_cases.title: Animate Existing Visual Assets
use_cases.description: Turn a product image, scene concept, or portrait into video using image-to-video and lipsync models.

use_cases.title: Prototype Video Pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine generation, upscaling, sound effects, and merging commands into repeatable media workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Video
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a simple text-to-video request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a five second AI video of [scene]. Use a fast model and explain the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this image URL: [image URL]. Choose an image-to-video model and use motion that matches [style or action].

prompt_templates.title: Create a Talking Avatar
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for avatar or lipsync workflows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking avatar video using [portrait URL] and [audio URL]. Pick a suitable lipsync model and describe any privacy considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Video Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for generation, enhancement, and merging.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a video pipeline for [goal]. Generate clips, add sound effects, upscale the result, and merge clips with a transition.

output_examples.input: Generate a forest flyover video with optional audio.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a Veo model, prepares an inference.sh command, and explains that the prompt will be sent to the remote model.

output_examples.input: Animate a portrait and sync it to a speech file.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends an avatar or lipsync model, asks for trusted media URLs, and prepares the CLI workflow.

output_examples.input: Merge two generated clips and add a fade transition.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses the media merger utility and describes the expected merged video output.

best_practices: Review each command before execution, especially when it references private media URLs.
Use the fastest suitable model for drafts, then switch to higher quality models for final renders.
Keep prompts, source files, and generated outputs organized for repeatable video production.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential images, audio, or unreleased campaign assets without approval.
Do not assume every listed model supports the same duration, resolution, audio, or cost profile.
Do not broaden Bash permissions beyond infsh commands without a new security review.

faq.question: What does this skill require?
faq.answer: It requires the inference.sh CLI, login access, and permission to run infsh commands.

faq.question: Can it generate video from text prompts?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes text-to-video examples for models such as Veo, Grok Video, Seedance, WAN-T2V, and P-Video.

faq.question: Can it animate an existing image?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents image-to-video workflows that accept image URLs for supported remote models.

faq.question: Does it process private files locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows use the inference.sh CLI and remote services, so users should treat inputs as externally processed.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it intentionally runs a networked CLI workflow.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
